verses.text: When Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem heard how Joshua had taken Ai and destroyed it (as he had done to Jericho and her king, so he had done to Ai and her king), and how the inhabitants of Gibeon had made peace with Israel and were among them, they feared greatly, because Gibeon was a great city, as one of the royal cities, greater than Ai, and all its men were mighty. Adoni-zedek sent unto Hoham king of Hebron, Piram king of Jarmuth, Japhia king of Lachish, Debir king of Eglon: Come up unto me, help me, that we may smite Gibeon: for it hath made peace with Joshua and the children of Israel. The five kings of the Amorites — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon — gathered together, went up, they and all their hosts, encamped before Gibeon, made war against it. Guidance: Adoni-zedek means 'lord of righteousness' — an echo of Melchizedek's 'king of righteousness' (Gen 14), but this Jerusalem king is no priest of God. He sees Gibeon's surrender as the more dangerous threat: if a major Canaanite city can switch sides, the whole coalition unravels. So five kings come not to fight Israel but to punish Gibeon. They forget that those who war against God's allies war against God.

The men of Gibeon sent unto Joshua to the camp to Gilgal: Slack not thy hand from thy servants; come up to us quickly, save us, and help us: for all the kings of the Amorites that dwell in the mountains are gathered together against us. Guidance: Last week Gibeon was lying through its teeth; this week it cries 'save us, help us' to the very people it had deceived. And Israel, having sworn by the LORD, must come. A treaty made under deception still binds when its other party calls in the dark. Mercy honors covenant beyond convenience.

Joshua ascended from Gilgal, he, and all the people of war with him, and all the mighty men of valour. The LORD said unto Joshua: Fear them not: for I have delivered them into thine hand; there shall not a man of them stand before thee. Guidance: Joshua moves first, then God speaks. Sometimes the obedience comes before the promise. The march is already up the hill country when the LORD adds, 'Fear them not.' God's reassurance reaches the leader who is already on the road, not the leader still on the couch debating.

Sometimes the obedience comes before the promise. The march is already up the hill country when the LORD adds, 'Fear them not.' God's reassurance reaches the leader who is already on the road, not the leader still on the couch debating.

Joshua came unto them suddenly: he went up from Gilgal all night. The LORD discomfited them before Israel, slew them with a great slaughter at Gibeon, chased them along the way that goeth up to Beth-horon, smote them to Azekah and unto Makkedah. As they fled from before Israel, and were in the going down to Beth-horon, the LORD cast down great stones from heaven upon them unto Azekah, and they died: more died with hailstones than they whom the children of Israel slew with the sword. Guidance: An all-night uphill march of about 20 miles, attacking by surprise at dawn. Then the second wave: hailstones, divinely aimed — falling on the fleeing enemy, sparing the pursuing Israelites. God's sword swings harder than Israel's. The numbers ratio of 'more died by hail than by sword' is the chapter's quiet boast: Israel's part in the victory is a fraction.

Then spake Joshua to the LORD in the day when the LORD delivered up the Amorites before the children of Israel; he said in the sight of Israel: Sun, stand thou still upon Gibeon; Moon, in the valley of Ajalon. The sun stood still, and the moon stayed, until the people had avenged themselves upon their enemies. Is not this written in the book of Jasher? So the sun stood still in the midst of heaven, and hasted not to go down about a whole day. There was no day like that before it or after it, that the LORD hearkened unto the voice of a man: for the LORD fought for Israel. Guidance: Joshua prays in poetic Hebrew, with all of Israel listening — public, bold, aimed at the heavens themselves. The narrator marks the day as unique in all history: 'there was no day like that.' God listens to a man speak to the sun. Whatever the mechanism, the meaning is clear: the cosmos itself rearranges to give Israel one more hour of light. When the LORD is fighting, time bends to finish the job.

Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, unto the camp to Gilgal. Guidance: A previewing summary — the chapter will return to this same line in v43. The narrator sets up Gilgal as the anchor: every campaign starts and ends at the place where the reproach was rolled away. Victory does not relocate the camp; it returns to the altar that named it home.

These five kings fled, hid themselves in a cave at Makkedah. It was told Joshua, The five kings are found hid in a cave. Joshua said, Roll great stones upon the mouth of the cave, set men by it to keep them. Stay ye not, but pursue after your enemies, smite the hindmost of them; suffer them not to enter into their cities: for the LORD your God hath delivered them into your hand. Guidance: Joshua refuses to chase the leadership while the army still escapes. Seal the cave with stones, post sentries, keep going. A general's discipline: do not let the satisfaction of capturing the five high-value targets cost you the broader victory. The deserving punishment can wait; the fleeing soldiers cannot.

When Joshua and the children of Israel had made an end of slaying them with a very great slaughter, till they were consumed, the rest which remained entered into fenced cities. All the people returned to the camp to Joshua at Makkedah in peace: none moved his tongue against any of the children of Israel. Guidance: 'None moved his tongue' — no taunt, no curse, no dog barking. The land falls silent. The Canaanites' boast that fueled them yesterday is gone overnight. When God fights, the loudest house on the block goes quiet first.

Joshua said: Open the mouth of the cave, bring out those five kings unto me out of the cave. They brought forth those five kings unto him: king of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon. When they brought out those kings unto Joshua, Joshua called for all the men of Israel, said unto the captains of the men of war which went with him: Come near, put your feet upon the necks of these kings. They came near, put their feet upon the necks of them. Joshua said: Fear not, nor be dismayed, be strong and of good courage: for thus shall the LORD do to all your enemies against whom ye fight. Guidance: A staged object lesson. Joshua calls every captain forward — they must put their feet on the necks themselves. The lesson is not just visual but personal: the same boot that will fight tomorrow is on the neck of yesterday's terror today. And the gospel of Joshua 1 returns: 'be strong and of good courage.' The promise that opened the book is now flesh-and-blood evidence.

Joshua smote them, slew them, hanged them on five trees: they were hanging upon the trees until the evening. At the time of the going down of the sun, Joshua commanded, they took them down off the trees, cast them into the cave wherein they had been hid, laid great stones in the cave's mouth, which remain until this very day. Guidance: Five trees, five hangings — a public sentence under the open sky. And again, Deuteronomy 21:23 is honored: down before sunset. The cave that hid them becomes their tomb; the stones they rolled in front of themselves earlier (v18) now seal them in. Sin has a way of providing its own grave-marker.

That day Joshua took Makkedah, and smote it with the edge of the sword, and the king thereof he utterly destroyed, them, and all the souls that were therein; he let none remain: he did to the king of Makkedah as he did unto the king of Jericho. Then Joshua passed from Makkedah, all Israel with him, unto Libnah, and fought against Libnah: the LORD delivered it also, and the king thereof, into the hand of Israel; he smote it with the edge of the sword, and all the souls that were therein. Joshua passed from Libnah unto Lachish, and encamped against it, and fought against it: the LORD delivered Lachish into the hand of Israel, which took it on the second day. Then Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish; and Joshua smote him and his people, until he had left him none remaining. Guidance: The narrator moves at march-pace now. Four cities in four verses. Makkedah (where the kings hid), Libnah, Lachish (two days — the only city that resists more than one), Gezer's king Horam (a foreign king who comes to the rescue and dies for it). The repeated phrase is 'the LORD delivered.' Israel is moving, but God is the verb of every sentence.

From Lachish Joshua passed unto Eglon, all Israel with him; they encamped against it, fought against it: took it on that day, smote it with the edge of the sword, and all the souls that were therein he utterly destroyed that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish. Joshua went up from Eglon unto Hebron, fought against it: took it, smote it with the edge of the sword, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof, and all the souls that were therein; he left none remaining. Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to Debir; fought against it: took it, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof; smote them with the edge of the sword, utterly destroyed all the souls that were therein. Guidance: Three more cities, three more days — Eglon, Hebron, Debir. The text speeds up to convey overwhelming success. The kings who were so confident at the start of the chapter are gone with their cities, their dynasties, their gods. The Amorite coalition assembled at v3 is dismantled town by town. Notice the place names: Hebron is where Abraham bought a burial cave 600 years before. The promise made to a wandering patriarch is now being collected, one city at a time.

From Lachish Joshua passed unto Eglon, all Israel with him; they encamped against it, fought against it: took it on that day, smote it with the edge of the sword, and all the souls that were therein he utterly destroyed that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish. Joshua went up from Eglon unto Hebron, fought against it: took it, smote it with the edge of the sword, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof, and all the souls that were therein; he left none remaining. Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to Debir; fought against it: took it, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof; smote them with the edge of the sword, utterly destroyed all the souls that were therein. From Lachish Joshua passed unto Eglon, all Israel with him; they encamped against it, fought against it: took it on that day, smote it with the edge of the sword, and all the souls that were therein he utterly destroyed that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish. Joshua went up from Eglon unto Hebron, fought against it: took it, smote it with the edge of the sword, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof, and all the souls that were therein; he left none remaining. Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to Debir; fought against it: took it, and the king thereof, and all the cities thereof; smote them with the edge of the sword, utterly destroyed all the souls that were therein. Guidance: Three more cities, three more days — Eglon, Hebron, Debir. The text speeds up to convey overwhelming success. The kings who were so confident at the start of the chapter are gone with their cities, their dynasties, their gods. The Amorite coalition assembled at v3 is dismantled town by town. Notice the place names: Hebron is where Abraham bought a burial cave 600 years before. The promise made to a wandering patriarch is now being collected, one city at a time.

So Joshua smote all the country of the hills, the south, the vale, and the springs, and all their kings: he left none remaining, but utterly destroyed all that breathed, as the LORD God of Israel commanded. Joshua smote them from Kadesh-barnea even unto Gaza, and all the country of Goshen, even unto Gibeon. All these kings and their land did Joshua take at one time, because the LORD God of Israel fought for Israel. Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, unto the camp to Gilgal. Guidance: The geographical sweep: Kadesh-barnea (where the spies failed forty years ago) to Gaza (Philistine coast). 'At one time' — one campaign settles the south. The reason is given without varnish: 'the LORD God of Israel fought for Israel.' Chapter closes where it had opened previewed: back at Gilgal. The day-long sun has set; the camp around the altar is the same. Conquest does not move the heart; the heart's address remains the place of grace.
